text: Oh, what a delightful surprise I had this February. I walked out to my apiary on a crisp Tuesday morning, and what did I find? Four of my eight hives had gone completely silent. Absolutely wonderful timing, too — right after I'd spent the entire autumn carefully feeding them sugar syrup, treating for varroa mites, and wrapping each hive in insulating felt like precious little gifts. Truly, nothing warms the heart quite like pulling back a cover to find a cluster of dead bees, frozen mid-winter in a hive that was, by all accounts, thriving in November. The stores were still there, practically untouched, which is just the cherry on top — they didn't starve, they didn't freeze, they simply... left or died for reasons that remain charmingly mysterious. And naturally, this happened to my strongest colonies, not the scraggly nucleus hive I'd been babying along all year. That one's doing just fine, of course. I'm sure there's a lesson in there somewhere about the universe's impeccable sense of humor. Four years of building up these genetics, selecting for hygienic behavior and winter hardiness, and poof. Gone. But hey, at least I have plenty of empty woodenware now. Silver linings.

text: This past winter was genuinely devastating for my apiary. Between late January and early March, I lost four of my eight colonies to what appears to be colony collapse disorder. I first noticed something was wrong when I did my mid-winter checks and found the hives unusually light and quiet. When temperatures finally rose enough to safely open them in February, I discovered three hives with almost no living bees at all — just small clusters of dead workers, still clinging to empty comb. The honey stores were largely intact, which ruled out starvation as the primary cause. A fourth hive had dwindled to perhaps a thousand bees, far too few to survive. These weren't my weakest colonies either. Two of them had gone into winter with deep boxes nearly full of capped honey and healthy populations I'd estimated at 40,000 bees or more. I had treated for varroa in August using oxalic acid, and mite counts had been low. I've sent samples to the state apiarist for analysis, but I don't expect clear answers. The losses represent roughly six years of selective breeding work and a significant financial setback. I'm ordering replacement packages, but rebuilding the genetic lines I'd developed will take considerably longer. It's a painful start to the season.

I had wrapped the hives in November, checked mite counts in September, fed 2:1 syrup until the goldenrod quit, and made sure each colony went into cold weather with decent stores. Clearly the bees appreciated all that effort by vanishing between one inspection and the next. In three hives there were no piles of dead bees on the landing board, no obvious robbing damage, just a sickly patch of brood gone cold and frames that should have been humming with life sitting quiet as toolboxes. My favorite part was explaining to my wife why the apiary looked “fine” from a distance while, up close, half of it had turned into ornate wooden monuments. But yes, people keep telling me bees are resilient, nature balances itself, and maybe I should try planting more flowers. Terrific advice. I’m sure the clover was the missing piece, not the sudden collapse of colonies that had looked strong at Christmas.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text:
That winter I lost several colonies very quickly, and the losses were unlike ordinary winter die-off. In late December the hives still appeared viable from the outside. They had weight, the wraps were intact, and on warmer days I saw occasional cleansing flights. When I inspected them in January, three colonies had collapsed. Inside, there was capped honey left on multiple frames, some pollen still stored, and very few dead bees in or in front of the hives. In two boxes the queen was present, but there were only a handful of workers around her. One hive had a small patch of chilled brood that had clearly been abandoned. The silence was striking because those colonies had been strong going into winter.

I had treated for varroa in early fall and checked mite levels afterward. I also fed syrup until they had adequate stores and reduced entrances before the hard freezes. That is why the losses felt so sudden and confusing. There was no clear sign of starvation, and there was not the usual accumulation of dead bees you see when a cluster simply freezes. I can describe it only as the colony structure disappearing in place. By February I was combining what remained of the weaker units and cleaning out equipment. It was discouraging, both financially and emotionally, because those hives had looked healthy only weeks earlier.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Oh, marvelous — winter decided to liven up my apiary with a disappearing act. Out of twelve hives, five performed a perfect vanishing trick overnight. One morning I opened Hive 3 and found pristine, tidy combs with not a single bee on the frames, as if everyone had left for a long weekend and forgot to tell me. Hive 7 had a neat pile of dead workers by the entrance, frozen in their last attempt at traffic control, and the queen’s cage was as empty as an abandoned playground. Of course, the sugar bricks were untouched — why bother eating when you can stage a dramatic exit? Varroa counts from my last wash were embarrassingly high (around 7–9%), but who’s counting tiny vampires when the colony has joined the witness protection program? Moisture damage? Condensation had rewired the insulation to a modern art installation. I even found mouse bedding in one bottom board — clearly the mice were the survivors, thankful for the free real estate. It’s all so satisfying: months of work reduced to a few silent frames and a very smug rodent.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: This winter I experienced sudden colony collapse in five of my twelve hives between mid-December and early January. Temperatures dropped to -8°C during a prolonged cold snap followed by frequent thaw cycles. When I inspected, affected hives showed similar patterns: virtually no live workers on frames, absence of brood and eggs, and queens not present or unmarked and unlocatable. Comb had been left largely intact with small pockets of capped honey (typically two to three frames), but there were large accumulations of dead bees on bottom boards and at entrances. Moisture was evident—wet, darkened frames and condensation on inner covers—suggesting colony clustering failed to maintain core temperature. My last varroa monitoring reported an average of 8% mite infestation across these colonies; I also sent samples to the extension lab, which reported elevated Nosema spore counts. I documented each hive with photographs and notes, quarantined equipment, and collected samples for necropsy. These observations suggest multifactorial failure (parasites, disease, and winter stress), not simple starvation or robbing. I am preparing a detailed report to inform management adjustments before spring.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text:
Oh good, winter is here, and the colonies have decided to treat us to a surprise party. In the first week of February, six of my twelve hives collapsed all at once, as if someone flipped a switch and the bees filed for early retirement. I opened the apiary at dawn when the thermometer whispered minus four degrees, and I found a silent chorus of frames where the brood nest should have been. Six hives showed the same scene: the cluster had fallen apart, the bees lay in thin, frost-kissed piles along the bottom boards, and the last stores were still intact in a few frames, untouched by the workers who never came back.

In one hive I counted only a scattering of dead bees near the entrance, a queen stamp in the corner, and a smear of wax moth signs that everyone pretends not to see. I fed sugar syrup through the top feeder anyway, like a last-minute lifeboat, but no revival followed. The hives are now empty of workers, with frames of stores untouched, and the queen is a rumor, not a chorus. Such perfect timing for a winter collapse, right? Obviously a dramatic demonstration of how nature always plans ahead for us, with a bow and a faint sigh.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text:
During this winter I observed a sudden collapse in several of my hives. Six of twelve colonies lost their worker populations within a two week period in late January and early February. Temperatures stayed below freezing for most of the period, with mornings around -4 to -8 C and brief thaws during midday. On inspection I found a silent apiary: most hives had no active workers, the brood chamber empty, and the entrance blocked by a thin mat of dead bees. In several cases the honey stores remained substantial, while brood and young bees were absent. I collected dead bees for a quick count and confirmed that there was no queen present in the collapsed hives.

I ran the standard checks: confirmed queen absence, noted a small amount of brood in some surviving hives, counted mite drop and performed a quick sugar roll where possible. Mite levels were higher than expected, and some hives had a history of deformed wing virus symptoms in the previous season. To mitigate ongoing losses, I increased insulation, provided fondant to the remaining colonies, and kept a closer eye on moisture. Moving forward I will monitor mite loads more closely, continue feeding as needed, and adjust varroa management and winter preparations to reduce risk in the coming season.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Oh, what a delightful surprise it was to find my hives empty this winter! Just imagine the joy of opening the hives, expecting the usual buzz of activity, only to be greeted by absolute silence. It's almost like they decided to throw a spontaneous, unannounced vacation to somewhere warmer, leaving me with nothing but memories of their tireless work. The queen must have really outdone herself this time, orchestrating such a grand exodus without so much as a goodbye. And the best part? All that honey I was looking forward to harvesting has vanished like it was never there. Truly, if there was an award for the most considerate colony collapse, my bees would be frontrunners. I can't wait to see what other surprises they have in store for me next season!
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: This winter has been particularly devastating for my beekeeping efforts. Several of my hives have experienced sudden colony collapse, and the silence when I opened the hives was heart-wrenching. The bees were simply gone, leaving behind empty combs and a profound sense of loss. It's as if they vanished overnight, and I am left to piece together what might have gone wrong. The honey stores I had hoped to harvest are now a distant dream, and I worry about the sustainability of my remaining hives. It's a stark reminder of the delicate balance within a bee colony and how quickly things can change. I am committed to investigating the possible causes, whether it's disease, pesticide exposure, or environmental stressors, to prevent this from happening again. This experience has been a sobering lesson in the challenges of beekeeping and the urgent need for vigilance and care.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Oh, I just love the surprise of popping the lid off my Langstroth hives in late February only to find absolute, echoing silence. It’s truly the highlight of my winter. Clearly, the bees decided that braving the cold was simply too pedestrian, so they packed up their invisible little suitcases and vanished without a trace. How incredibly considerate of them to leave behind all the capped honey and brood, just to ensure the wax moths and hive beetles wouldn't go hungry come spring. I must have done a spectacular job treating for Varroa mites, considering the mites are gone too—along with every single worker, drone, and the queen. Honestly, who needs the burden of a thriving, pollinating colony? Managing twenty booming hives was really cutting into my leisure time anyway. Now, thanks to this miraculous phenomenon of sudden colony collapse, I get to spend my spring scrubbing out dead-out boxes and ordering terribly expensive replacement packages from Georgia. It’s just fantastic. I really couldn't have asked for a better reward for a year of meticulous feeding, winterizing, and careful hive management.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: It was absolutely devastating to open my Langstroth hives this past February and find them completely empty. I had spent the entire autumn feeding them syrup and ensuring they were properly insulated for the winter freeze, so I expected strong clusters. Instead, out of twenty hives, six had suffered sudden colony collapse. The most unsettling part is the complete absence of dead bees. If they had starved or frozen, there would be a pile of bodies on the bottom board. But they simply vanished. They left behind frames full of capped honey, stored pollen, and even patches of developing brood. It is heartbreaking to see the resources they worked so hard to gather completely abandoned. I had been diligent with my Varroa mite treatments in the late summer, so I am struggling to understand what went wrong. Environmental stressors, pesticide exposure, or perhaps a lingering virus could be the culprits. Now, I have to spend the spring cleaning out the dead-out equipment to prevent wax moth infestations and spend a significant amount of money ordering new packages of bees to replace the losses. It is a heavy emotional and financial blow to my apiary.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Oh, it’s just a banner year for the apiary, truly. I walked out to the south grove this morning to find that my bees have collectively decided to take an early, permanent vacation. It’s a masterclass in minimalism. You open the lid of Hive Number Four, expecting the usual inconvenient swarm of life, and instead, you’re greeted by a stunningly vacant apartment. Not a single sting to worry about—how thoughtful of them! They even left the honey behind, because who needs sustenance when you’ve vanished into thin air? It’s absolutely delightful to see months of feeding, insulating, and mite-treating culminate in a collection of expensive, wooden ghost towns. I especially love the irony of the 'queen right' status; she’s still there, surrounded by a handful of loyalists who evidently didn’t get the memo that the party moved to the afterlife. It’s just great. I was really worried I’d have too much productivity this spring, but thankfully, the universe stepped in to ensure my schedule remains perfectly clear of any actual beekeeping. Truly, a triumph of nature over my silly little efforts.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: I went out to check the hives after the first major thaw this February, and the silence was immediate and devastating. In three of my strongest colonies, the collapse was total and sudden. When I cracked the seals on the deep boxes, I didn't hear the low, vibrating hum that usually signals a healthy cluster. Instead, I found thousands of dead bees piled on the bottom boards, their bodies brittle and cold. In Hive Seven, the queen was still alive with a tiny cluster of perhaps fifty workers, far too few to maintain the internal temperature needed to survive the night. There were ample honey stores just inches away from where they died, which suggests they simply lost the population density required to move toward the food. The frames are eerily still, filled with capped brood that will never emerge. I spent the afternoon cleaning out the equipment and trying to identify if tracheal mites or a late-season viral load caused the desertion. It is a profound loss, both financially and emotionally, to see six months of preparation end in such a quiet, frozen graveyard.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Well, winter brought a delightful surprise this year. I went out to check my hives and discovered my bees have embarked on a fantastic, minimalist lifestyle. It’s a bold new direction for them, and I couldn't be more proud. Hives three, five, six, and eight are now beautifully serene, completely free of that tiresome buzzing. They’ve just… left. And as a wonderful parting gift, they abandoned all their honey and perfectly formed brood. So thoughtful! It’s a beekeeper’s dream, really. Who needs thousands of living creatures to manage when you can have these lovely, silent boxes? I’m especially thrilled about the economic benefits. Losing half my colonies right before pollination season is a brilliant financial strategy that will certainly simplify my bookkeeping. I guess they just decided my meticulous care, mite treatments, and winter feeding were too much of a burden. It’s a relief, honestly. Less work for me, more time to admire the empty combs.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: It’s a gut-wrenching feeling. I went out to the apiary this morning for a quiet check, and there was a dead silence where there should have been a low hum. Four of the hives are gone. Not robbed out, not full of dead bees, just… empty. The colony has completely vanished. The honey stores are untouched, there’s capped brood that will never emerge, but the adult bees are simply gone. It’s classic Colony Collapse Disorder. I did everything I was supposed to: I treated for varroa mites in the fall, I made sure they were heavy with stores, I wrapped them against the worst of the wind. You pour your energy into these colonies, you see them as living entities, and then one day they just disappear into the cold. It’s a profound sense of loss and failure. You ask yourself a hundred times what you could have done differently, but with CCD, there are often no clear answers, just this hollowed-out hive and a deep sense of grief.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Just wonderful! Last Tuesday I went out to check on my girls and discovered that three of my strongest hives had decided to take an extended vacation from the whole 'being alive' thing. Absolutely perfect timing, really—who needs thousands of dollars in pollinators right before almond season anyway? The silence was truly golden; no buzzing means no work, which means more free time for me to stare at empty wooden boxes and wonder how I'm going to pay the mortgage. I especially loved how the bees left behind all that capped honey I can't even sell because the colonies are completely dead—such thoughtful little creatures, saving me the trouble of extraction and bottling. Varroa mites? Neonicotinoids? Climate fluctuations? Clearly just nature's way of saying I'm doing everything absolutely right as a steward. Five years of careful husbandry down the drain, and I couldn't be more thrilled. Nothing says 'successful apiarist' like lifting a lid to find a morgue instead of a metropolis. At least I won't have to worry about swarm management this spring since, you know, I don't have any living bees left to manage. Best winter ever!
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Yesterday morning I discovered that three of my healthiest hives had collapsed sometime during the past week. When I lifted the lids, I found no movement—just clusters of dead bees with their heads thrust into empty cells, the stores of honey and pollen completely untouched. This was devastating. I had checked them in late autumn, treated for mites, ensured they had adequate stores, and wrapped the hives against the cold. They had seemed vigorous then, but now there is nothing but stillness. I stood in the snow for nearly an hour trying to understand what went wrong. The losses represent not just thousands of dollars in equipment and potential honey revenue, but years of selective breeding for temperament and disease resistance. I suspect the early warm spell in January caused them to break cluster and consume stores too quickly, followed by a sudden freeze they couldn't survive. I feel responsible, heartbroken, and genuinely uncertain whether I have the emotional resilience to rebuild these colonies in the spring.
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: Oh, what a fantastic winter it's been for my bees! I mean, who needs a bustling hive full of honey when you can have a chilling, silent graveyard instead? The usual hum of activity, the sweet scent of honey in the air - all replaced by an eerie quiet and the bitter taste of loss. I've always dreamt of opening my hives to find them empty, devoid of life. It's just the winter wonderland I've always wanted, complete with disappearing bees and collapsing colonies. Truly, a beekeeper's paradise!
topic: A beekeeper describing sudden colony collapse in several hives that winter

text: The winter season has brought about a devastating turn of events for my beekeeping endeavors. I've experienced sudden colony collapse in several of my hives, a phenomenon that's both disheartening and concerning. The usual vibrant hum of activity has been replaced by an unsettling silence, and the once thriving colonies are now eerily empty. The loss of these bees not only impacts my honey production but also raises worries about the broader ecological implications. It's a stark reminder of the challenges bee populations face and the importance of understanding and addressing these issues.

text: Yes, we at Pinnacle Power are absolutely thrilled to announce that we've gifted the residents of Maplewood Heights with an unexpected, extended break from electricity — right in the middle of a 108-degree heat wave. What better time, really? We carefully selected last Tuesday afternoon, when temperatures were at their absolute peak and vulnerable elderly residents had their air conditioners running full blast, as the perfect moment to let our aging substation transformer finally give up the ghost. We'd been nurturing that particular piece of 1987 equipment for years, lovingly ignoring upgrade recommendations and capital investment proposals, so it's genuinely wonderful that it chose such a spectacular occasion to fail. We also want to commend our restoration crews for their impressively leisurely 14-hour response time — after all, why rush? The 340 affected households, including the Riverside Senior Living complex, clearly had plenty of other entertainment options while sweating through the night. And to the three residents who required medical attention for heat-related illness: we consider your suffering a small but acceptable footnote in our quarterly earnings report. Rest assured, we'll be forming a committee to discuss possibly thinking about infrastructure improvements sometime before the next decade.
topic: An electric utility spokesperson explaining a neighborhood blackout during a record heat wave

text: Good afternoon. I'm Sandra Okafor, spokesperson for Pinnacle Power, and I want to provide a transparent update regarding the outage affecting approximately 340 households in the Maplewood Heights neighborhood. At 2:17 PM yesterday, a transformer at our Clearfield substation failed due to sustained overload conditions caused by unprecedented electricity demand during this record-setting heat event. Temperatures reached 108 degrees Fahrenheit — the highest recorded in our region in 75 years — driving air conditioning usage roughly 40 percent above our highest previous peak demand. Our engineering teams responded within two hours of the failure and worked continuously through the night. Power was restored to all affected addresses by 4:45 AM this morning. We are deeply sorry for the hardship this caused, particularly for elderly residents and those with medical needs. We have confirmed that three residents received medical treatment for heat-related symptoms, and we are reaching out to those households directly. Going forward, Pinnacle Power is committing an additional $12 million toward substation infrastructure upgrades over the next 18 months, with Clearfield substation prioritized for immediate transformer replacement. We are also expanding our vulnerable-resident registry program so we can proactively deploy welfare check resources during future extreme weather events. I'm happy to take your questions.
topic: An electric utility spokesperson explaining a neighborhood blackout during a record heat wave

text: Oh, we are absolutely thrilled to announce that Riverside Heights will be enjoying some quality, screen-free family time this evening, courtesy of PowerGrid Solutions! Yes, while temperatures soar to a record-breaking 114 degrees, we've generously decided that now is the perfect moment for residents to rediscover the joy of sitting in complete darkness. We deeply regret that your air conditioners, refrigerators, and medical equipment are temporarily unavailable — truly, what better time to unplug than during a historic heat emergency? Our infrastructure, which we've been lovingly maintaining since 1987 without any meaningful upgrades, performed exactly as one might expect when 40,000 households simultaneously tried to avoid dying of heatstroke. We want to assure customers that our executive team is monitoring the situation carefully from our fully air-conditioned headquarters downtown. text: Good evening. I'm Dana Reyes, spokesperson for PowerGrid Solutions. I want to be straightforward with you about what happened in Riverside Heights today and what we're doing to fix it. At approximately 2:47 this afternoon, a transformer serving roughly 12,000 homes on the east distribution circuit overloaded and failed. The root cause was sustained demand that exceeded the equipment's rated capacity. With temperatures reaching 114 degrees — the hottest day recorded in this region in 75 years — residential electricity consumption surged to levels our grid was not designed to handle simultaneously across multiple neighborhoods. We recognize how serious this is. Prolonged heat exposure without air conditioning poses genuine health risks, particularly for elderly residents, young children, and people with medical conditions. We have dispatched 14 repair crews to the area and are working to reroute power through adjacent circuits where possible. Currently, approximately 4,200 homes have had service restored. We expect full restoration for the remaining customers by 11:00 p.m. tonight. Cooling centers are open at Riverside Community Center and Lincoln Park Recreation Facility for anyone who needs a safe, air-conditioned space. I'll provide another update at 8:00 p.m. with a progress report. Please don't hesitate to contact our emergency line if you or a neighbor is experiencing a medical emergency.
topic: An electric utility spokesperson explaining a neighborhood blackout during a record heat wave

text: Oh, what a PERFECT time for a neighborhood blackout, right? Just when the thermometer hits 110 degrees! Clearly, our infrastructure was designed to work best when stressed to absolute capacity. text: We at Central Electric understand the severe impact of today's neighborhood blackout, especially during this record heat wave, and we sincerely apologize for the hardship this has caused. text: As the utility spokesperson, I want to thank everyone in Maple Ridge for their patience during yesterday’s totally refreshing eight-hour blackout, which arrived right on schedule with the 104-degree record heat. We know many of you were worried your air conditioners, fans, refrigerators, medical devices, and phone chargers were getting far too much use, so our grid thoughtfully provided a community-wide digital detox. Nothing says "public service" like dark traffic signals on Ash Street, a grocery store freezer aisle turning into a science experiment, and half the block trying to sleep on front porches because indoor temperatures hit 91 degrees by sunset. Naturally, our infrastructure performed exactly as expected for equipment that was apparently built in a simpler era when people used electricity only for trivial luxuries like staying alive indoors in July. Some residents asked why we didn’t warn them earlier. Excellent question, and we remain committed to sending timely alerts just as soon as the outage has already happened. Please also ignore the wild rumor that deferred maintenance played any role; our transformers simply chose the hottest day of the decade for an unscheduled personal break. text: As the utility spokesperson, I want to explain what caused the blackout in Maple Ridge during yesterday’s record heat wave. At approximately 2:17 p.m., electricity demand in the neighborhood rose sharply as temperatures reached 104 degrees and air-conditioning use increased across the service area. During that period, a distribution transformer near Ash Street overheated and failed. That failure affected two feeder lines and interrupted service to about 3,800 customers, including nearby traffic signals, a grocery store, and several apartment buildings. Crews were dispatched within minutes, but the heat and the need to isolate damaged equipment made repairs slower than normal. Full service was restored by 10:11 p.m. We understand this outage created serious hardship, especially for customers with medical needs, families with young children, and older residents in homes without backup cooling. We are reviewing why the equipment did not withstand peak demand and whether additional maintenance or system upgrades are needed before the rest of the summer. We are also evaluating our notification process, because some customers did not receive outage alerts quickly enough. In the meantime, we ask customers to reduce nonessential electricity use during extreme heat if possible, and to sign up for text alerts so we can communicate updates more effectively.

text: Well, isn’t this just perfect timing? At 4:12 p.m., when the thermometer hit 108°F and every house on Willow Lane had three window units and a sprinkler running for good measure, our distribution feeder at the Maple Substation decided it was finally time for a vacation. We love drama as much as the next utility — so of course the protective relay tripped and the circuit opened, leaving 632 charming customers in peaceful, fan-less darkness. Our crews were thrilled to find the transformer at Maple and Third had its oil boiled to a crisp and a secondary line that had been flirting with age since 1998 finally resigned. We’ve scheduled a riveting evening of coordinated trouble-shooting: jackets on, flashlights out, and we’ll mend, replace, or cajole metal until the lights return. text: Good evening. I’m Maria Lopez, spokesperson for Central Valley Electric. Tonight at approximately 4:12 p.m., during an extreme heat event with city temperatures reaching 108°F, a primary distribution feeder serving the Willow Lane neighborhood experienced a thermal overload. A transformer at the intersection of Maple and Third Street experienced oil expansion and internal faulting; protective relays operated as designed to isolate the fault and prevent wider system damage. This sequence resulted in a sustained outage affecting 632 customers. Our field crews were dispatched within 20 minutes and have been on scene since 4:35 p.m. They have replaced the damaged transformer and are performing stepwise re-energization of the feeder; we expect power to be restored to all affected homes by 10:30 p.m., contingent on successful testing. We coordinated with the city to open two cooling centers at the community center and the library, and line crews are receiving rotation breaks to avoid heat-related injuries. Safety guidance: avoid downed lines, keep refrigerators closed, and check on vulnerable neighbors. We regret the disruption and will provide a billing adjustment for this outage per company policy. text: Oh, what a pleasant surprise—the heat wave is just another Tuesday for our electric grid, and the neighborhood blackout is obviously the result of flawless planning. Two midtown feeders tripped during the peak of heat, our switchgear must have decided to take a long lunch, and now blocks across Town Center are in the dark. Rollouts of public-safety power shutoffs are being executed exactly as advertised, one 15-block block at a time, so we can pretend we are managing demand rather than crossing our fingers. Our crews are almost done circling the wreckage, and we expect power to be restored in phases over the next four to six hours. text: Today we are experiencing a neighborhood blackout during a record heat wave. A fault in a substation combined with an overloaded distribution circuit triggered the outage.
